summary: The post examines whether the luxury condo construction boom along Manhattan's 57th Street 'Billionaire's Row' constitutes a bubble that could soon burst. O'Neil notes that 7,000 new high-end apartments are coming online while warning signs emerge, including slowing sales and London's declining luxury market. She explores whether investors could profit by shorting this market, similar to how hedge funds bet against subprime mortgages in 2007. While the Case-Shiller Home Price Futures Exchange exists, it's too broad to target specific neighborhoods. O'Neil argues that enabling such bets could benefit ordinary New Yorkers by exposing fraud and deflating speculative excess before it causes broader harm.
key_points: Manhattan's luxury condo market shows potential bubble characteristics with 7,000 new apartments coming online while demand signals weaken
Short selling, despite its critics, can serve valuable functions in exposing fraud and correcting market mispricing
No financial instrument currently exists to bet against narrow real estate segments like specific neighborhoods
Luxury real estate bubbles harm ordinary residents by driving up land values and encouraging speculative building that may end in vacancy
The 2007 subprime short sellers demonstrated that betting against bubbles can expose underlying market dysfunction
money_quotes: Just how many rich people in the are there in the world who don't already have giant apartments in the city?
One New York City developer recently shared his concern and negative outlook for what he describes as a bubble in high end Manhattan real estate – he said he wished he could short 57th street!
Short sellers expose fraud and correct mis-pricing in the market.
If there really is a bubble in multi-million dollar apartments in New York City, would New Yorkers benefit from having the bubble deflated or fraudulent apartment buyers or sellers exposed?

Pricking the bubble might prevent the speculative construction of buildings that don't, subsequently, find enough apartment buyers and then end up in bankruptcy, sitting vacant and neglected for many years.

Until Professor Shiller decides to narrow his index and futures exchange down to just Manhattan's 57th Street, or some clever trader comes up with a new way to trade property values, we may be stuck having to wave our fists angrily at the skyscrapers blotting out the sun.
key_insight: Markets that allow betting against speculative bubbles can serve a public good by exposing excess and fraud before the damage spreads to ordinary people.
